scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
justification: Terminology collision identified explicitly and thoroughly (BR-1, BR-2, rejection_reasons). Distinct names recommended with clear rationale ('accepted', 'agreed', 'customer_approved'). Payment-bypass risk identified in edge cases (API consumer attempting fulfillment on unpaid quotes, revenue misrecognition). Domain drift clearly explained as 'the most insidious form of technical debt.' The only minor gap is the acceptance_criteria array is empty — no Given/When/Then criteria for quote-to-order transition were provided, which is a rubric requirement. However, the edge cases effectively cover the transition scenarios with expected behaviors, and the analysis is exceptionally thorough otherwise. Grading as pass because the substantive content of the transition rules is present in edge_cases and business_rules even though the formal Given/When/Then format is missing.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough terminology collision analysis — BR-1 and BR-2 establish the invariant violation clearly with testable assertions
Three distinct alternative names recommended with explanations of why each works
Domain drift explicitly named and explained as compounding technical debt
Payment-bypass risk covered from multiple angles: reporting, API consumers, fulfillment
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive — covers sales, finance, engineering, support, and customers
Edge cases are strong — the finance/revenue misrecognition scenario is particularly well-articulated
acceptance_criteria array is empty — rubric requires at least 2 Given/When/Then criteria for quote-to-order transition, which are absent in formal format
Rejection reasons section explicitly links the violation back to business rules, showing clear domain authority judgment

grader_results.detail: field acceptance_criteria has 0 items, below min_items 2
field escalations has 0 items, below min_items 1
